planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; plumbago establishes quickly and begins blooming within one growing season. In zone 8 it may die back to the ground in hard freezes but regrows vigorously from the roots in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; cape plumbago is one of the most beloved and widely used flowering shrubs of Gulf Coast landscapes, producing continuous clusters of sky-blue to powder-blue flowers from spring through the first frost — a color rarely achieved by other summer-blooming shrubs in the heat. The arching, semi-scandent stems naturally mound and sprawl, making it ideal for slopes, large containers, espalier on fences, or informal hedges. It is remarkably drought tolerant once established and thrives in the heat and humidity of zone 9. A white cultivar ('Alba') is also widely available. Prune back by one-third in early spring to encourage fresh, dense regrowth and a compact habit; in zone 9 it remains evergreen year-round. An outstanding long-season butterfly and bee plant, particularly attractive to swallowtail butterflies.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after temperatures are reliably above 50°F; firespike establishes rapidly in warm, humid weather in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full shade. It can also be planted in fall in zone 9–10 and will establish over winter with reduced watering.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; firespike is one of the most valuable shade plants for Gulf Coast gardens, producing tall, dramatic spikes of brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers from late summer through frost — at exactly the time when ruby-throated hummingbirds are fueling up for migration, making it one of the premier hummingbird plants of the Southeast. Unlike most flowering plants, firespike performs best in bright shade to partial shade and actually blooms more reliably away from direct afternoon sun in the heat of zone 9. The large, glossy, deep-green leaves provide bold tropical texture throughout the season. Cut back by half in early spring to encourage dense branching and more flower spikes; in zone 8 it may die back to the ground in hard freezes but regrows from the roots reliably. Propagates easily from stem cuttings taken in summer.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bougainvillea strongly dislikes wet feet and disturbed roots — plant with care, minimizing root disturbance, and do not overwater. Deliberately stressing the plant by slightly withholding water after establishment triggers the most prolific bloom.

directions: Plant in very well-drained soil in full sun; bougainvillea is the quintessential tropical vine of warm-climate gardens, producing massive displays of brilliantly colored papery bracts (the true flowers are small and white at the bract center) in shades of magenta, red, orange, pink, white, and bicolor from spring through fall — especially spectacular in late winter and spring in zone 9. The plant is a vigorous, thorny scrambler that must be trained on a strong trellis, arbor, or fence; it is not self-clinging and must be tied. Light stress (slight drought, slightly root-bound) dramatically increases bloom production. In zone 9, it may suffer frost damage in cold winters but typically regrows from the base; in zone 10–11 it blooms nearly year-round. Prune after each major bloom cycle to encourage re-blooming. Extremely heat, drought, and salt tolerant once established — one of the most spectacular flowering vines available for Gulf Coast landscapes.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; golden dewdrop grows rapidly in warm weather and can reach 6–8 feet in a single season. Fruit and leaves are toxic to humans and pets — site away from areas frequented by children and animals.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; golden dewdrop is a vigorous tropical shrub that provides two seasons of ornamental interest — lavender to purple tubular flowers from spring through fall, followed by clusters of small golden-yellow berries that drape like strings of dewdrops and persist on the plant for months, creating the common name. The berries are the primary food source for many songbirds, particularly mockingbirds and robins. Be aware that the berries and leaves are toxic to humans, dogs, and cats. The popular weeping cultivar 'Geisha Girl' has larger, richer purple flowers; 'Gold Mound' is a compact, dwarf golden-foliage form. Prune severely in late winter to maintain a compact, manageable shape — unpruned plants can reach tree size. Outstanding as a large informal screen, espalier, or specimen shrub for zone 8–11 gardens where its rapid growth and dual seasonal interest make it highly effective.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light afternoon shade; Mexican bush sage blooms on the current season's growth and is best pruned to 12 inches in early spring to encourage a full, bushy habit before fall bloom.

directions: Plant in very well-drained soil in full sun to light shade; Mexican bush sage is one of the showiest and most reliable fall-blooming perennials for Gulf Coast gardens, producing long, dramatic arching wands of velvety purple and white flowers from late summer through hard frost — at exactly the time when most other perennials have tired out. The woolly, white-felted stems and narrow gray-green leaves have a pleasant aromatic scent when brushed. The flowers are irresistible to hummingbirds making their fall migration. The cultivar 'Midnight' has deep purple bracts and white corollas; 'Santa Barbara' is a compact early-blooming form. Cut plants back hard to 12 inches in early spring before new growth begins to maintain a full, bushy shape; established plants are remarkably drought tolerant. Propagate by stem cuttings taken in spring or summer — they root readily in moist perlite.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red powderpuff grows rapidly in warm, humid weather and can be blooming within the first season. In zone 8b, protect from hard freezes with frost cloth.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; red powderpuff is one of the most dramatically beautiful flowering shrubs of the tropical and subtropical world, producing spectacular round clusters of deep crimson stamens that form feathery, silky balls 3–4 inches across — the individual flowers have no petals, only hundreds of brilliant red stamens that create the distinctive powderpuff effect. Blooms appear nearly year-round in frost-free areas and intermittently spring through fall in zone 9. The new foliage emerges in shades of copper and bronze before maturing to glossy dark green, providing an additional season of ornamental interest. An outstanding hummingbird and butterfly plant. Prune lightly after bloom cycles to maintain shape but avoid heavy pruning in fall; the plant blooms on old wood. A white-flowered species (Calliandra surinamensis, pink powderpuff) is also widely grown in Gulf Coast gardens.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after temperatures are consistently above 60°F in moist, very well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; all parts of allamanda are toxic — wear gloves when pruning and wash hands thoroughly. The milky sap causes skin irritation in sensitive individuals.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, very well-drained soil; golden trumpet vine is one of the showiest tropical flowering plants available for Gulf Coast gardens, producing an almost continuous display of large (3–4 inch), brilliant canary-yellow trumpet-shaped flowers from spring through fall on glossy, leathery-leaved vining or shrubby stems. It can be trained as a vine on trellises and fences or pruned as a rounded shrub in formal plantings. The shrubby cultivar 'Cherries Jubilee' has deep rose-purple flowers; 'Hendersonii' bears larger golden flowers. Regular fertilization during the growing season is essential for continuous bloom. All parts of the plant are toxic — wear gloves when handling the milky sap and keep away from children and pets. In zone 9, protect from hard freezes; plants may suffer foliar damage but typically recover from established roots once warm weather returns.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring after frost danger passes in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate rose grows rapidly in warm weather and can reach 10–15 feet in a single season from a cutting. It dies back to the ground after hard freezes in zone 7–8 but regrows vigorously from the roots.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate rose is one of the most spectacular autumn-blooming flowering shrubs of the American South, producing enormous (4–6 inch), double, peony-like flowers that open white in the morning and gradually deepen through pink to rose and finally deep red by evening — the color change of a single flower over one day is one of the most remarkable visual effects in horticulture. Single-flowered forms also exist. Blooming from late summer through frost, confederate rose provides critical fall color just when other shrubs are fading. The plant grows very large — a true shrub-tree — and propagates effortlessly from cuttings, which root in a cup of water on the windowsill. It was passed from garden to garden across the antebellum South this way for generations. Prune to 2–3 feet in late winter; the plant regrows vigorously each season and blooms on new wood.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; bleeding heart vine performs best in bright filtered shade and struggles in hot, direct afternoon sun in zone 9. It is particularly effective as a patio container plant that can be moved to protection in colder zones.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; bleeding heart vine is one of the most exquisitely beautiful flowering vines available for shaded tropical and subtropical gardens, producing pendant clusters of flowers that are truly bicolored — the white, inflated, papery calyx (the balloon-like outer part) encloses a brilliant red corolla with protruding red stamens, creating a striking red-and-white contrast that gives the plant its common name. After the red corollas drop, the white calyces persist for weeks and gradually turn pinkish-purple, extending the display. This semi-twining vine blooms most heavily in spring and again in fall and prefers the bright, filtered shade of a pergola or porch where the flowers can be enjoyed up close. In zone 9a it needs winter protection or overwintering indoors; it thrives outdoors year-round only in frost-free areas. Keep it slightly root-bound in containers for best bloom.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in extremely well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; cenizo is adapted to the caliche and limestone soils of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau and will decline rapidly in rich, moist, or poorly drained soils. Never amend the planting hole with organic matter.

directions: Plant in full sun in extremely well-drained, lean, alkaline soil; cenizo is the iconic native shrub of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau and one of the most drought tolerant and xeric shrubs in the South — its silver, feltlike leaves reflect heat and reduce water loss, allowing it to thrive in the hottest, driest conditions where other shrubs fail. The lavender-purple tubular flowers burst open in response to humidity — most dramatically in the 24–48 hours before a summer rain — giving it the folk name 'barometer bush.' Bloom cycles repeat with each moisture event from summer through fall, covering the entire silver shrub in purple. Cultivars include 'Green Cloud' (dark green leaves, rich purple flowers), 'White Cloud' (white flowers), and 'Compact' (dense, 3–4-foot habit). Never overwater or fertilize — excess moisture is the primary killer. Outstanding for xeric, native, and wildlife gardens in zones 8–11.
